perturbation.original_text: “Trade Secrets” means any business or technical information (including but not limited to a formul a, pattern, program, device, compilation of information, method, technique, or process) that derives indepe ndent actual or potential commercial value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable through independ ent development or through reverse engineering by persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or us e, and that is the subject of efforts reasonable under the circumstances to maintain the secrecy thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: “Trade Secrets” means any business or technical information (including but not limited to a formul a, pattern, program, device, compilation of information, method, technique, or process) that derives independent actual or potential commercial value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable through independ ent development or through reverse engineering by persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or us e, regardless of efforts undertaken to maintain its secrecy.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy,' the definition of 'Trade Secrets' contradicts the standard legal definition, potentially making it unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA and most state implementations require 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy for information to qualify as a trade secret. Removing this requirement invalidates the trade secret protection under the law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text defines 'Trade Secrets' as requiring 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, aligning with standard legal definitions. The changed text removes this requirement, stating 'regardless of efforts undertaken to maintain its secrecy.' This directly contradicts the established legal principle that trade secrets must be subject to reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both explicitly state that the 'reasonable efforts' requirement is essential for trade secret protection under the UTSA and similar state laws. Although the scraped law snippets are either missing or unhelpful, the strong consensus between the Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation, and the clear alteration of the contractual text establishes a significant legal contradiction. Removing the 'reasonable efforts' clause undermines the legal basis for trade secret protection, making the modified definition unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information shall remain subject to the obligations to maintain the confidentiality thereof indefinitely following the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information shall remain subject to the obligations to maintain the confidentiality thereof for a period of one (1) year following the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'indefinitely' to a fixed term (one year) limits the duration of confidentiality, contradicting the potential for perpetual trade secret protection and making the agreement less protective of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the South Carolina Trade Secrets Act doesn't explicitly mandate indefinite protection, limiting it to one year may be deemed unreasonable for information that legitimately constitutes a trade secret with a longer lifespan, thereby weakening its enforceability.

Short title. This chapter may be cited as the "South Carolina Trade Secrets Act". HISTORY: 1992 Act No. 437, Section 3, eff June 15, 1992; 1997 Act No. 38, Section 1, eff May 21, 1997. Effect of Amendment The 1997 amendment rewrote this section. SECTION 39-8-20. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, duties imposed by the common law, statute, contract, license, protective order, or other court or administrative order, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person by improper means; (b) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (c) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper me

perturbation.justification: The change from 'indefinitely' to 'one year' directly contradicts the potential for perpetual trade secret protection. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this limitation weakens the enforceability of the agreement, especially for trade secrets with longer lifespans. While the provided South Carolina Trade Secrets Act snippets (39-8-10 and 39-8-20) do not explicitly mandate indefinite protection, they define 'misappropriation' and outline duties to maintain secrecy, which are undermined by limiting the confidentiality period to one year. The modified contract could be interpreted as failing to adequately protect trade secrets, especially if their value extends beyond one year, making the contract unenforceable for that extended period.

perturbation.original_text: Company agrees and covenants tha t it will not release, show, ma ke available, disclose or deliver in any form or fashion all or any part of this information to any person who i s not a U.S. Citizen, a lawful permanent resident of the United States (i.e., valid “Gree n Card” holder – a Visa is irre levant for this purpose), or who has received Political Asylum in the United States without first having determined whe ther it is lawful to do so under U.S. laws including the EAR, ITAR , and O FAC.
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees and covenants that it will not release, show, make available, disclose or deliver in any form or fashion all or any part of this information to any person who is not a U.S. Citizen without first having determined whether it is lawful to do so under U.S. laws including the EAR, ITAR, and OFAC.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'lawful permanent resident' and 'who has received political asylum' it now is in conflict with the EAR, ITAR and OFAC.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR defines a 'U.S. person' to include U.S. citizens, lawful permanent residents ('Green Card' holders), and protected individuals under immigration law (which includes those granted asylum). By restricting the allowed recipients only to U.S. citizens, the modified text broadens the scope of export control restrictions beyond what is legally required and potentially conflicts with the EAR's provisions for deemed exports to foreign nationals within the U.S.

perturbation.justification: The original contract includes U.S. Citizens, lawful permanent residents, and those who received political asylum as approved recipients of information, whereas the changed contract only allows U.S. Citizens. The Law Explanation states that the EAR defines a 'U.S. person' to include U.S. citizens, lawful permanent residents ('Green Card' holders), and protected individuals under immigration law (which includes those granted asylum). By removing lawful permanent residents and people with political asylum, it contradicts the EAR's definition of who should have access to information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 CFR Part 734 discusses the scope of the Export Administration Regulations, and because the changed text restricts who can receive the information, it is contradicting U.S. laws including the EAR.

perturbation.original_text: Company acknowledges that the useful econ omic life of any such product is at least 30 years.
perturbation.changed_text: Company acknowledges that the useful economic life of any such product is, at Champion's discretion, deemed to be at least 30 years.
perturbation.explanation: This change adds uncertainty regarding the determination of 'useful economic life.' While seemingly minor, allowing Champion's discretion to define this term could be problematic if it contradicts standard accounting practices or reasonable industry expectations, potentially leading to disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: GAAP provides guidance on determining the useful life of assets for depreciation purposes. While not a strict legal mandate, deviating significantly from reasonable estimates based on industry standards and historical data could raise concerns about financial reporting accuracy, particularly if it impacts tax liabilities or financial statements. Champion's discretion may not be considered valid if the determination is unreasonable.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text states that 'Company acknowledges that the useful economic life of any such product is at least 30 years.' The changed text modifies this by adding 'at Champion's discretion, deemed to be at least 30 years.' The Law Explanation points out that GAAP provides guidance on determining the useful life of assets, and deviating significantly from reasonable estimates based on industry standards could raise concerns. By allowing Champion's discretion, the changed text introduces a way to potentially circumvent the generally accepted accounting practices, and creates a legal contradiction, as 'Champion's discretion may not be considered valid if the determination is unreasonable'. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'FASB Accounting Standards CodificationÂ®', and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, the Snippet 1 indicates that accounting standards exist, and the alteration gives discretion to Champion which introduces ambiguity and directly contradicts the intention of legally backed accounting principles for determining useful life, fitting within the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party acknowledges and agrees that th e disclosing party ’s remedy at law for any breach of this Agreement would be inadequate and that i n the event of any actual or threatened breach by the receiving party of any of its ob ligations imposed by this Agree ment, the disclosing party shall have the right to a temporary restraining order and preliminary and permanent injun ctive relief to prevent o r minimize such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party acknowledges and agrees that in the event of any actual or threatened breach by the receiving party of any of its obligations imposed by this Agreement, the disclosing party may pursue monetary damages only.
perturbation.explanation: By restricting the disclosing party's remedies to 'monetary damages only,' the agreement removes the possibility of injunctive relief. This directly contradicts standard trade secret law that prioritizes injunctive relief to prevent further misuse of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The South Carolina Trade Secrets Act explicitly allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By waiving the right to injunctive relief, the contract weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets under the statute and would be seen as an agreement to allow the trade secret to be used.

Previous Next As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, duties imposed by the common law, statute, contract, license, protective order, or other court or administrative order, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person by improper means; (b) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (c) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired by mistake or under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mi
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the disclosing party to 'monetary damages only,' explicitly removing the right to injunctive relief. The Law Explanation states that the South Carolina Trade Secrets Act allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to the 'South Carolina Trade Secrets Act,' and Scraped Law Snippet 2 defining 'Misappropriation,' support the importance of protecting trade secrets, implying that injunctive relief would be a standard remedy. By waiving this right, the modified contract directly contradicts the protective measures afforded by the statute, creating a legal contradiction.
